# oazapfts adapter (Mode B overlay example)

A reference **adapter** that makes calls made through an [oazapfts](https://github.com/oazapfts/oazapfts)
-generated OpenAPI SDK client visible to zzop's cross-layer analysis — without adding any
oazapfts-specific vocabulary to the engine.

## The problem it solves

oazapfts generates one thin fetch wrapper per OpenAPI operation:

```ts
// generated by oazapfts — a real call site from an oazapfts-generated SDK
export function getActivities(albumId: string, opts?: Oazapfts.RequestOpts) {
  return oazapfts.ok(
    oazapfts.fetchJson<{ status: 200 }>(`/activities${QS.query(QS.explode({ albumId }))}`, { ...opts })
  );
}

export function createActivity(activityCreateDto: ActivityCreateDto, opts?: Oazapfts.RequestOpts) {
  return oazapfts.ok(
    oazapfts.fetchJson<{ status: 201 }>('/activities', oazapfts.json({ ...opts, method: 'POST', body: activityCreateDto }))
  );
}
```

The route and verb are both statically visible — but only via a call shape (`oazapfts.fetchJson(url,
opts)`, with the verb sometimes hiding inside an `oazapfts.json(...)`/`oazapfts.multipart(...)` wrapper
and the URL sometimes carrying a trailing oazapfts-specific query-string helper interpolation) that is
specific to one code generator's output, not a general HTTP client API.

## Why this is an adapter, not engine vocabulary

This call family used to be recognized **natively** by zzop's TypeScript egress extractor
(`parser/parser-typescript/src/adapters/egress.rs`'s `oazapfts-v1` recognizer). Per zzop's "generated
SDKs = injection adapters" decision, that recognition has been retired from the engine and reproduced
here instead — the same reclassification `../react-query-adapter/` (a cache-key convention) and
`../openapi-sdk-adapter/` (any other OpenAPI-generated SDK, keyed via `operationId`) already went
through for their respective app-specific idioms. The engine stays neutral about which OpenAPI code
generator, if any, a frontend happens to use; the oazapfts shape now lives only in this adapter.

## What it recognizes

Exactly the call family the retired native recognizer matched:

- `oazapfts.fetchJson(url, opts?)`, `oazapfts.fetchText(url, opts?)`, `oazapfts.fetchBlob(url, opts?)` —
  the receiver identifier must be **exactly** `oazapfts` (`client.fetchJson(...)` on any other receiver
  is not recognized; there is no bare-method-name allowlist).
- `url` (the first argument): a string literal or a template literal. Template `${...}` interpolations
  collapse to zzop's own `{}` route-param placeholder — **except** a *trailing* interpolation (nothing
  but the closing backtick after it) whose expression text starts with `QS.` (oazapfts's query-string
  helper, e.g. `` `/activities${QS.query(QS.explode({ albumId }))}` ``), which is **dropped entirely**
  (contributes nothing, not `{}`) so the query part never pollutes the key. A `QS.` interpolation that is
  *not* the trailing piece still collapses to `{}` like any other interpolation.
- Method: `GET` by default, overridden by a literal `method: "..."` property found either directly in the
  2nd-argument options object, or inside an `oazapfts.<helper>({ ... })` wrapper call passed as the 2nd
  argument (`oazapfts.json(...)`, `oazapfts.form(...)`, `oazapfts.multipart(...)`, or any other helper
  name — the wrapper is recognized by its `oazapfts.` receiver, not a fixed helper allowlist, matching
  the retired native recognizer). The value is upper-cased.
- A call nested inside `oazapfts.ok(...)` is still detected — `ok(...)` is not special-cased; the scan
  simply finds `oazapfts.fetchJson(...)`/etc. wherever it textually appears.
- Emitted consume: `kind: 'http'`, `client: 'oazapfts'`, `key` = `METHOD <normalized path>` via
  `adapter-kit`'s `resolveConsumeKey` (the same internal/external/base-relative dispatch the retired
  native `consume_key_for` used).

## Usage

```sh
node adapter.mjs --root <tree-root> [--source web] > overlay.json
```

Pass the resulting envelope through your zzop config's `adapterOverlays` array (a field on the
`analyze`/`analyzeTrees` request):

```js
const overlay = JSON.parse(execSync(`node adapter.mjs --root web --source web`));
const out = JSON.parse(native.analyzeTrees(JSON.stringify({
  trees: [
    { root: 'web', sourceId: 'web', adapterOverlays: [overlay] },
    { root: 'api', sourceId: 'api' },   // native NestJS/Express/etc. provides, no overlay needed
  ],
})));
```

## Limitations (a production adapter can go further)

- **Lexical, not a real AST.** Call sites are found by scanning the whole file's text for
  `oazapfts.fetchJson`/`fetchText`/`fetchBlob`, then using a bracket/quote/template-literal-aware
  balanced scanner to find each call's argument list and read inside its 2nd argument for `method:`.
  This is a step beyond the single-line regex the other reference adapters in this repo use (needed here
  because method resolution has to look *inside* a — possibly wrapped — options object, not just match a
  call site's leading argument), but it is still not a real parser: no scope/type analysis, no
  handling of unusual formatting the scanner's bracket/quote/comment/template tracking doesn't cover.
- **Request-body shape is not captured.** The retired native recognizer additionally witnessed a
  sibling `body:` property's statically-visible literal shape (`IoConsume.body`, `body-shape-v1`). This
  adapter does not reproduce that: `adapter-kit`'s `EnvelopeBuilder.addConsume` only accepts
  `kind`/`key`/`line`/`raw`/`method` — it has no `body` option — and extending the shared kit is out of
  scope for this adapter (this adapter must not modify `adapter-kit`). A `body:` property next to
  `method:` is simply ignored; the consume is still emitted with its resolved key and method, just with
  no body-shape evidence attached. See `test/adapter.test.mjs`'s last test for the exact behavior this
  produces.
- **`client: 'oazapfts'` is attached by post-processing the built envelope**, for the same reason as the
  body-shape limitation above — `EnvelopeBuilder.addConsume` has no `client` option either. This is
  still exact (not an approximation): every consume this adapter can possibly emit came from an
  `oazapfts.*` call site, so tagging every emitted consume unconditionally is correct.
- Generic type arguments between a method name and its call's opening paren (`oazapfts.fetchJson<{
  status: 200 }>(...)`) are skipped via a simple `<`/`>` depth count — sufficient for the type-literal
  shapes oazapfts' own generated clients emit, but not a real type-expression parser.
- A URL argument that is not a bare string/template literal (e.g. a variable, a concatenation) is never
  guessed — the call site is skipped and counted in the stderr summary, same "only emit from visible
  literals" convention every adapter in this repo follows.
- Test-file exclusion and the walked extension set (`.ts`/`.tsx`/`.js`/`.jsx`/`.mjs`) match the other
  reference adapters' defaults (`adapter-kit`'s `walk`).

## Relationship to the retired native recognizer

This adapter's 8 `test/adapter.test.mjs` cases are direct ports of the `oazapfts-generated-SDK call
family` test block that used to live in
`parser/parser-typescript/src/adapters/egress.rs` (before its removal), covering: the trailing-`QS.`
suffix drop, method resolution from both `oazapfts.json(...)` and `oazapfts.multipart(...)` wrappers,
detection through an `oazapfts.ok(...)` wrapper, a path-param template placeholder (`fetchBlob`), a
mid-path (non-trailing) `QS.` interpolation staying a `{}` placeholder, and a non-`oazapfts` receiver
correctly going unrecognized — plus one adapted case (the body-shape limitation above) for the one piece
of native behavior this adapter does not reproduce.
